entry.guide_markdown:
## Saline County, Missouri — Locality Guide (1856–1910)

**Standard place:** Saline, Missouri, United States. County seat: Marshall. Formed 1821 from Howard County; no boundary changes in target period.

### Census
- 1860 (98% indexed, imageGroup 005170226_003_M9CV-6H8)
- 1870 (97% indexed, imageGroup 004273849_001_M9CN-M42)
- 1880 (indexed nationally via FS/Ancestry)
- 1890 population census DESTROYED; Veterans Schedule survives (98% indexed, imageGroup 004679097)
- 1900 (95-97% indexed, imageGroups 004118864_002_M92X-W2Q, 004118865_001_M9KY-X4M)

### Vital Records
Birth registration began 1909 (observed from ~mid-1910s). Death registration began 1883 (sparse until ~1895). Pre-registration births/deaths: church records and family bibles.
- FS collections 1680833, 2524491 (births/christenings)
- FS collections 1680837, 2448947 (deaths/burials)
- Missouri State Archives birth/death database: sos.mo.gov

### Marriage
Multiple FS volumes covering 1835-1910, 48-65% indexed + full-text searchable:
- Vol. A 1835-1874 (007513914)
- Vol. D 1875-1881 (007513915)
- Vols. 1-2 1881-1887 (007513916)
- Vols. 3-4 1887-1894 (007513953)
- Vols. 5-6 1894-1902 (007514068)
- Vols. 7-8 1902-1910 (007514069)
Also searchable via collection 2060668 (7.8M records).

### Church Records
- Catholic, Marshall (St. Peter's): imageGroup 008133031 — baptisms/marriages/deaths 1871-1954, full-text searchable, 0% name-indexed
- Shackleford: imageGroup 008116687 — 1852-1954, browse-only
- Malta Bend: imageGroup 008474084 — 1860-1899, browse-only
- Religious birth records Marshall area 1871-1876: imageGroup 007966224, full-text searchable

### Probate
Multiple volumes 1854-1922, all 0% name-indexed but full-text searchable:
- F-G 1854-1866 (007636435), H-I 1866-1871 (007636436), J-K 1871-1876 (007636437)
- L-M 1876-1884 (007630169), N 1884-1886 (007630168)
- Wills A-B 1837-1881 (007630167), C-D 1881-1907 (007630166)
- General index 1821-1891 (007636433)

### Military
- Confederate service records: FS 1932374
- Union service records: FS 1932408
- Confederate pension applications: FS 1865475

### Local History
- Saline County History 1881 (imageGroup 007940688, full-text searchable)
- Missouri Obituaries 1880-1990: FS collection 4373182 (584K records)
- Find A Grave, Missouri Gravestone Photo Project

entry.rationale: Source QQK2-QZ8 in tree.gedcomx.json references the 1880 US Census entry for 'Geo. Broyles and Elizabeth Broyles' in Marshall, Saline, Missouri — the direct household record for Charles Robinson Broyles's father and mother at the time of Charles's birth (Dec 1878). This record is not yet extracted into research assertions. Extracting it will enumerate all children in the household and establish the core roster of siblings. The 1880 Census is 98% name-indexed on FamilySearch (imageGroup 004118864). If the source ARK does not resolve, search collection 6742 (1880 US Census) for George/Geo Broyles in Saline County, MO.

The 1870 Census (3 years after George's second marriage to Mary Elizabeth Davis ~1867) will show any children born between the marriages (1856-1867 gap) or early in the second marriage, who may have grown and left home before the 1880 census. It also documents whether any children from the first wife Liddy/Sidney Shoemaker were still in George's household after remarriage. Saline County 1870 is 97% indexed (imageGroup 004273849_001_M9CN-M42). Search for George Broyles / Geo Broyles in Saline Co., MO.

Source S26H-D5M in tree.gedcomx.json references the 1860 US Census entry for George Broyles and Sidney Broyles in Palestine Township, Cooper County, Missouri — George's household during his first marriage. Children listed there would be half-siblings of Charles Robinson Broyles via his father. Source is already in tree but not extracted into assertions. The 1860 Census is 98% indexed. Extracting this source will identify the half-sibling roster from the first marriage (George + Liddy/Sidney Shoemaker, married 1856).

Extract and search the Missouri County Marriage records (collection 2060668) for Broyles family members marrying in Saline County 1880-1910. Source QYVY-Y9B in tree.gedcomx.json shows 'Joseph S Vandyke and Judie A Broyles' married 1 Sep 1886 — Judie A. Broyles is a strong sibling candidate. Additional Broyles marriage records in this period may identify further siblings. Saline County marriage volumes 1881-1910 are 48-65% indexed and full-text searchable on FS. Search for 'Broyles' as groom/bride surname in Saline Co.

Three tree sources hint at daughters of George Broyles who relocated to Texas: (1) 3SS4-B5C — Texas Deaths 1890-1977 entry for Myrtle Leana Cartmill (d. 16 Jan 1970); (2) 7MDD-6LP — Texas Deaths 1890-1977 entry for Minnie Bell Strickland (d. 5 Nov 1971); (3) QYVY-TNY — SS NUMIDENT for 'Minnie Broyles and George Broyles.' These records are already in tree.gedcomx.json but unextracted. Death certificates name parents; if Myrtle and Minnie name George Broyles and Mary Elizabeth Davis as parents, they are confirmed full siblings of Charles. If they name George Broyles and Sidney/Liddy Shoemaker, they are half-siblings.

If Myrtle Leana Cartmill and Minnie Bell Strickland were daughters of George Broyles who migrated to Texas, the 1900 and 1910 censuses would show their households and confirm birth years and birthplaces consistent with a Saline County, MO origin. Cross-referencing against the 1880 household roster establishes sibling relationships. Search for Myrtle Broyles/Cartmill and Minnie Broyles/Strickland in Texas 1900-1910.

If George Broyles (LCPC-PZK) died in Missouri, his probate record would name his heirs — typically his surviving children — providing a definitive sibling list. Saline County probate volumes for 1876-1907 are on FamilySearch (imageGroups 007636437, 007630169, 007630168, 007630166, 007630167) and are full-text searchable. Also check the general probate index 1821-1891 (imageGroup 007636433). Search fulltext for 'Broyles' in Saline County probate records.

The Missouri Obituaries collection (4373182, 584K records, 1880-1990) may include obituaries for George Broyles or his children listing surviving relatives by name. Obituaries frequently name siblings. Also search the 1881 Saline County local history (imageGroup 007940688, full-text searchable) for a Broyles family biography that may list children. These contextual sources satisfy BCG Standard 14 (topical breadth) and may corroborate or extend the sibling roster established by census records.

The 1900 Census (95-97% indexed, Saline Co., imageGroups 004118864_002_M92X-W2Q and 004118865_001_M9KY-X4M) would show George Broyles's household 20 years after the 1880 census, enumerating any children still at home and listing how many children George's wife had borne and how many were still living. Charles Robinson Broyles (b. 1878) would be ~21 and may be enumerated separately. Search for George Broyles in Saline County 1900 as a corroborating and extending search for siblings not yet identified.

entry.independence_analysis: The SS NUMIDENT derives from Minnie's own SS-5 application filed October 1939; the Texas death certificate reflects a family informant's report in November 1971, ~32 years later. The two documents were created independently by different persons for different purposes (benefit enrollment vs. death registration). However, both informants may have drawn on the same family oral tradition about Minnie's birth date, which reduces their independence on this specific point. They are treated as substantially independent — neither is derived from the other — but neither can claim primary-information status for a birth date ~62–94 years earlier.
entry.weighing_analysis: Both sources are secondary information about the birth date: Minnie could not have primary knowledge of her own birth (she was a newborn), and the 1971 family informant was not an eyewitness. The SS self-report (Feb) carries the advantage of being Minnie's own statement about herself, but it was made 62 years post-event and could reflect imperfect family oral tradition rather than documentary knowledge. The death-certificate informant (Oct) was further removed: an unnamed family member reporting in 1971, 94 years after the event, with no reason to prefer October over February beyond what they were told or believed. Critically, both the day (28) and year (1877) agree across both sources — a strong indicator that the underlying family tradition is substantially correct and the discrepancy is a single-element transcription error or memory lapse in one source. Without a primary source (birth certificate, church baptism register, or family bible), neither month can be preferred. The 1880 census shows Minnie's age as 3 (implying birth in 1876–1877) — consistent with 1877 but too coarse to resolve the month. The conflict cannot be definitively resolved with available evidence.
entry.resolution_rationale: Unresolvable with current evidence. The month discrepancy (February vs. October) cannot be adjudicated on available sources alone. To resolve, the original SS-5 form (not the NUMIDENT summary) should be requested from the Social Security Administration, and the original Texas death certificate image should be examined to confirm the informant's statement. Any family bible, church baptismal record, or Missouri birth registration from 1877 (rare before statehood mandate of 1909) would provide authoritative evidence. The conflict is noted honestly per GPS Element 4 but does not block q_001 — Minnie's identification as a full sibling of Charles Robinson Broyles (b. 9 Dec 1878) rests on her father (George Broyles LCPC-PZK) and mother (Mary Elizabeth Davis LCPC-PDH) as named in multiple concordant sources; the birth month is not material to that conclusion.

entry.narrative_markdown:
## Proof Summary: Siblings of Charles Robinson Broyles (MJ7L-SL9)

### Research Question

What children were enumerated in the household of George Broyles (LCPC-PZK, born about 1830 Tennessee, residing Marshall, Saline County, Missouri) in the 1880 federal census, and which of those children are siblings of Charles Robinson Broyles (MJ7L-SL9, born 9 December 1878)?

### Conclusion

Charles Robinson Broyles had at least five siblings through two of his father's marriages. Three were **full siblings** through George Broyles's second wife Mary Elizabeth Davis: **Judy A. Broyles** (born about 1868, later Vandyke), **Minnie Bell Broyles** (born 1877, later Strickland), and **Myrtle Leana Broyles** (born 17 May 1886, later Cartmill). Three were **half-siblings** through George Broyles's first wife Sidney Shoemaker (KP3V-5CY): **William H. Broyles** (born about 1858), **Geo. W. Broyles** (born about 1861), and **Milton M. Broyles** (born about 1859). This conclusion is rated **probable**: all accessed sources are derivative indexes rather than original images, one conflict in Minnie's birth date remains unresolved (c_001), and Milton M. Broyles's identity rests on a single source.

### Evidence and Analysis

#### Anchor Record: 1880 Federal Census

The central record for this conclusion is the 1880 United States Census, which enumerates the household of Geo. Broyles in Marshall, Saline County, Missouri:

> "United States, Census, 1880," FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:M6F6-6VT : accessed 2026-07-23), entry for Geo. Broyles household, Marshall, Saline County, Missouri.

The census lists George Broyles as head, wife Elizabeth Broyles (born 1851, Missouri), and five children: Wm. H. Broyles (son, born 1858, Missouri), Geo. W. Broyles (son, born 1861, Missouri), Judy A. Broyles (daughter, born 1868, Missouri), Minnie Broyles (daughter, born 1877, Missouri), and Chas. Broyles (son, born 1879, Missouri). The youngest child, Chas. Broyles (born 1879, Missouri), is identified with Charles Robinson Broyles (MJ7L-SL9, born 9 December 1878) — the convergence of name, birth year, birthplace, and FamilySearch's source attachment to MJ7L-SL9's profile leaves no reasonable doubt. This record directly places Charles in the same household as four siblings and establishes their co-residence as of June 1880.

#### George Broyles's Two Marriages: Establishing the Sibling Classification

The evidence shows George Broyles married twice, producing two sets of children. The earlier marriage is documented in a Missouri marriage record:

> "Missouri, County Marriage, Naturalization, and Court Records, 1800-1991", FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:WGTK-9P6Z : accessed 2026-07-23), entry for George Broyles and Miss Sidney Shoemaker, 18 May 1856.

This record establishes George Broyles's union with Sidney Shoemaker (KP3V-5CY) beginning in 1856. The 1860 federal census documents the first family:

> "United States, Census, 1860," FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:MHZ5-ZV6 : accessed 2026-07-23), entry for George Broyles household, Palestine Township, Cooper County, Missouri.

The 1860 census shows George Broyles with wife Sidney Broyles (born 1839, Missouri) and two sons: W H Broyles (born 1858, Missouri) and Milton M Broyles (born 1859, Missouri). These children — William H. and Milton M. — are thus confirmed as children of George and his first wife Sidney Shoemaker, making them half-siblings of Charles Robinson Broyles. Geo. W. Broyles (born about 1861) appears in the 1880 census as George's son; his birth year falls within the period of the Shoemaker marriage (1856 through approximately 1866–67, before the children of Mary Elizabeth Davis begin appearing), classifying him as a half-sibling by inference from birth chronology. No record explicitly names Sidney Shoemaker as Geo. W.'s mother; this classification rests on the elimination of Mary Elizabeth Davis as his mother (her children are born 1868 and later).

By 1880, the household wife is Elizabeth Broyles (born 1851, Missouri), identified from death certificate evidence as Mary Elizabeth Davis. The Davis children — born 1868 and later (Judy A., Minnie, and Charles) — are full siblings of Charles.

#### Corroborating Evidence for the Davis Children

**Myrtle Leana Cartmill (I5):** The Texas death certificate directly names her parentage:

> "Texas, Deaths, 1890-1977," FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:KSY2-LWQ : accessed 23 July 2026), entry for Myrtle Leana Cartmill, 16 January 1970; citing Texas Department of Health, Austin.

The certificate records her birth as 17 May 1886 in Marshall, Missouri, father George Broyles, mother Mary Elizabeth Davis. Though secondary evidence (reported at death, 84 years after the fact), this record independently corroborates Myrtle's parentage and identifies the second wife's full name as Mary Elizabeth Davis. Myrtle is not enumerated in the 1880 census (born 1886) but is established as a sibling of Charles based on shared parentage.

**Minnie Bell Strickland (I4):** Two independent records corroborate Minnie's parentage. The Social Security NUMIDENT:

> "United States, Social Security Numerical Identification Files (NUMIDENT), 1936-2007," FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:6K7V-X4BQ : accessed 23 July 2026), entry for Minnie Broyles Strickland; NUMIDENT database derived from SS-5 application filed October 1939.

names father George Broyles and mother Elizabeth Davis, and places her birth in Marshall, Saline County, Missouri. The Texas death certificate:

> "Texas, Deaths, 1890-1977", FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:K36Y-YFJ : accessed 2026-07-23), entry for Minnie Bell Strickland and Broyles, 5 November 1971.

gives her birth as 28 October 1877 in Missouri, father's surname Broyles. Both records are consistent with the 1880 census enumeration of Minnie Broyles (born 1877). **A conflict exists:** the NUMIDENT gives the birth date as 28 February 1877 while the death certificate gives 28 October 1877. Both are secondary evidence; neither can be preferred over the other, and this conflict (c_001) remains unresolved. It does not affect the sibling relationship — both records consistently identify Minnie as George Broyles's daughter born in Missouri circa 1877.

The 1896 Saline County marriage record further corroborates Minnie's identity:

> "Missouri, County Marriage, Naturalization, and Court Records, 1800-1991," FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:6XWX-JNLS : accessed 23 July 2026), entry for Dee Z Zumwalt and Minnie Broyles, 14 Apr 1896.

**Judy A. Broyles (I3):** The 1886 Saline County marriage record documents Judy's lineage:

> "Missouri, County Marriage, Naturalization, and Court Records, 1800-1991", FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:6XF4-YVCP : accessed 2026-07-23), Entry for Joseph S Vandyke and Judie A Broyles, 1 Sep 1886.

This record names her father as George Broyles, directly corroborating her status as George's daughter and, by extension, as a full sibling of Charles Robinson Broyles.

#### Conflicting Evidence

One conflict was formally identified and entered as c_001: the birth date of Minnie Bell Strickland (I4). The SS NUMIDENT (a_063) gives 28 February 1877; the Texas death certificate (a_074) gives 28 October 1877. Both records are secondary evidence created decades after the birth event. The NUMIDENT was self-reported by Minnie herself in 1939; the death certificate was reported by an informant at death in 1971. Both may draw on the same family oral tradition and neither can be demonstrated to be more reliable. Primary birth records for Missouri in 1877 were not accessible to resolve the discrepancy. The conflict is limited to the birth month and does not affect the parentage or sibling conclusion.

### Limitations and GPS Assessment

1. **Derivative sources throughout.** All records accessed are FamilySearch derivative indexes. No original images were examined. Index transcription errors cannot be ruled out, though no specific error was detected.

2. **Unresolved birth-date conflict (c_001).** The Minnie birth month conflict between S3 and S4 cannot be resolved with available evidence. This prevents a "proved" GPS rating for the overall question.

3. **Milton M. Broyles (I6) rests on a single source.** The 1860 federal census is the only record locating Milton in the Broyles family. No later record corroborates his continued relationship to the household. His identity as a half-sibling of Charles is probable but not independently verified.

4. **Geo. W. Broyles (I2) maternal classification is inferred.** No record explicitly names Sidney Shoemaker as Geo. W.'s mother. The half-sibling classification rests on birth chronology: his birth circa 1861 falls within the Shoemaker marriage period and before the earliest Davis child (Judy A., born about 1868).

5. **Missouri vital records were not accessible.** Civil registration of births in Missouri began in 1883 but compliance was incomplete through the 1890s; even if birth records existed for the Broyles children, they were not found in accessible form during this research. Such records, if located, could provide primary direct evidence of parentage.

### Sources Cited

1. "United States, Census, 1880," FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:M6F6-6VT : accessed 2026-07-23), entry for Geo. Broyles household, Marshall, Saline County, Missouri.
2. "Texas, Deaths, 1890-1977," FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:KSY2-LWQ : accessed 23 July 2026), entry for Myrtle Leana Cartmill, 16 January 1970; citing Texas Department of Health, Austin.
3. "United States, Social Security Numerical Identification Files (NUMIDENT), 1936-2007," FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:6K7V-X4BQ : accessed 23 July 2026), entry for Minnie Broyles Strickland; NUMIDENT database derived from SS-5 application filed October 1939.
4. "Texas, Deaths, 1890-1977", FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:K36Y-YFJ : accessed 2026-07-23), entry for Minnie Bell Strickland and Broyles, 5 November 1971.
5. "United States, Census, 1860," FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:MHZ5-ZV6 : accessed 2026-07-23), entry for George Broyles household, Palestine Township, Cooper County, Missouri.
6. "Missouri, County Marriage, Naturalization, and Court Records, 1800-1991", FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:6XF4-YVCP : accessed 2026-07-23), Entry for Joseph S Vandyke and Judie A Broyles, 1 Sep 1886.
7. "Missouri, County Marriage, Naturalization, and Court Records, 1800-1991," FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:6XWX-JNLS : accessed 23 July 2026), entry for Dee Z Zumwalt and Minnie Broyles, 14 Apr 1896.
8. "Missouri, County Marriage, Naturalization, and Court Records, 1800-1991", FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:WGTK-9P6Z : accessed 2026-07-23), entry for George Broyles and Miss Sidney Shoemaker, 18 May 1856.

# Mentor review: proof-critique on ps_001

Good work here. You resisted the temptation to call this 'Proved' — the narrative openly names the unresolved c_001 birth-month conflict and Milton M. Broyles's single-source status as the reasons you stopped at 'probable.' That is Standard 43 in action, and it's the harder, more honest call. The narrative is also genuinely self-contained: I could follow the whole argument and find every source without touching research.json.

## What to address before moving on

There is one real gap in the analysis, and it involves Geo. W. Broyles (I2). Your narrative classifies him as a half-sibling (child of Sidney Shoemaker, not Mary Elizabeth Davis) purely by chronology-elimination — his birth year (~1861) falls before Judy A.'s (~1868), so he's assigned to the first marriage. But assertion a_028, drawn directly from the 1880 census, is on the books as 'child of Elizabeth Broyles' with evidence_type marked 'direct.' Your proof silently overrides that assertion without ever treating it as a conflict, and — more importantly — without ever confirming the actual date George married Mary Elizabeth Davis. The 'elimination' you describe rests entirely on Judy A.'s estimated census age, not on a documented marriage date. No search for a George+Mary Elizabeth Davis marriage record appears anywhere in the log.

This is the same kind of independence/weighing work you did carefully for c_001 (Minnie's birth month) — it just wasn't done here. I'd like to see either a formal conflict entry for I2's maternal parentage (addressing a_028 head-on) or a targeted search for that second marriage record, which would settle the question outright.

## Worth considering

All eight sources came from FamilySearch, and your own locality guide notes that Saline County's marriage and probate volumes are 0% name-indexed there — full-text search only. That's precisely the situation where Ancestry's independently built index could surface a hit FamilySearch missed, including the still-unlocated Davis marriage record. Worth a pass before you consider this question fully closed.

## What would change my mind

A conflict entry for I2 (or its sibling records) that names a_028 explicitly, gives an independence and weighing analysis the way c_001 does, and either prefers the census assertion or the chronology inference with reasons — or, better, a located marriage record fixing George's remarriage date — would resolve this. Short of that, I'd want the proof to at least flag I2's classification as 'possible' rather than folding him into the same 'probable' tier as the well-corroborated full siblings.
